speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. And we were talking about liberation. I'd like to go back, and I'd like you to tell me more about that when it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So when we were liberated in Montauban-- you know, and I told you I was in the convent and the Americans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '44, May, when the war-- when they came in. You know, I keep forgetting dates, but I think it was '44 or '45 when we were liberated in Montauban-- I mean, when I was in Montauban, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first hear of them coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when I was in the convent, we didn't get any radio listening time there. So somebody told us that the Germans-- that the Americans have landed, you know, like, June 6th. So we-- we were told the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then when we were liberated, after I left the convent-- actually, I stayed there and I asked Mother Superior if I could go out and find a Jewish organization. So apparently, the bishop guided me to an organization in Montauban, a Jewish organization. And they told me that I should leave the convent now, that they would take over where I would go and what I would do now-- Which organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: --at this point. That was the-- it was part of the OSE. And also, like I mentioned to you, the joint, [? Hyas-- ?] that was a New York-based organization that helped throughout the country. A Jewish organization that raised money for these type of things, for homes for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hyas, I think, is H-Y-A-S. So that's what it was called. And I found the organization, and at that point, they placed me with a Jewish family who apparently survived the war or whatever. I didn't know very much about them. They were not the most congenial people. They kind of took advantage of me a little bit. So I guess after a while, I complained about it, and then they moved me someplace else to another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what they were like and their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took advantage of me. For instance, we had-- we had shortages of food. So the only way-- and after the war, you couldn't get anything. And so they would send me, like, 4:00 in the morning to the marketplace to queue up to bring home food. I mean, they did to me what they were not supposed to do. Like I was their errand boy. And they told-- that's when I finally complained. I should have complained sooner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember their name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently, I blocked it out. I was there maybe two months, two and a half months. And then they put me in another place, and it's like a blank to me today. I know I went to another place that they sent me, and it was a good place. And then I got little odd jobs in Montauban until I could find my uncles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I went back to Paris. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was going to different homes. I went to one home from the OSE near Paris that the OSE sent me to. And it was a home-- it was a lovely home. And it was part of a chateau. It was a big, gorgeous chateau-- Chateau de Ferrieres near Paris. It was owned by the Rothschild family. And they were-- we were told that some more kids were coming. We didn't know what they were talking about. Boys were coming, and we got the homes ready. What had arrived were young men who were in camp and were liberated, and they were sent to our home. They were not little children we were-- we thought that would come to us. So we worked with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what camps they were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were-- they came from Auschwitz, from Buchenwald. They came from all different camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of condition were they in? Did you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had been in the GP camps in Germany. So at that time, they already had eaten-- had food already. And then they made sure that our home was supplied with food-- ample food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they talk to you about their experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we talked about, but, you know, we had a hard time communicating because they spoke all different languages. There were Hungarians, there were Poles, there were Germans, there were Russian kids-- all different languages. So-- and we had teachers on the ground to teach them French. And it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting back, did you know what was happening in the concentration camps throughout your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes! Oh, yes, we knew. Absolutely we knew at that point. And that's where I really started to worry at this point now because of my family. I hadn't heard from my parents for all the years since 1943. So sure, you knew what had happened because you had messengers, you had people come and tell you these stories, what had happened. And then these boys came, and they were all survivors of these camps. And-- but it was interesting to watch them in the beginning because they were-- almost, they were raised in the camps. They were like animals, when they were smaller, fighting over a piece of bread. So when they came, they were stealing some bread and trying to steal things. And finally, the director explained to them in a good way. He says, look. You get all the food you want. You don't have to steal a piece of bread. If you want another piece of bread, just ask us. In a good way, in a nice way he told them that you don't really have to do that. And then we had little, funny incidents. We all went to the movies, into Paris, and-- and with their broken French. But we all got-- we all got along. The kids, really, they did very well. These young men did quite well. And, like I told you, we lived in this beautiful chateau, but we had only the downstairs, like, the basement. And then there was another house where only the boys stayed. And the girls were in the basement of the main chateau. It's magnificent.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, I decided I really want to see-- I'd never been to a chateau-- what it looks like upstairs. So I went up the back way. I found stairwells, and I opened the door. Was sort of cranking, an old door-- made lot of noise. And I walk in, and I look, and I see beautiful paintings. And all of a sudden, there's a man greeting me. And he says, hello, what's your name? I told him. I still was Fanny.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, well, I've never been in a chateau, and I really wanted to see what it looks like. So he took me by the hand and he showed me around. And then I found out it was Baron [INAUDIBLE] Rothschild. It was him. It was the baron.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then later, he would horseback ride through his property, through the yard to the beautiful garden. And he came out one day, and I was walking around with my friends. And he turned around to me, he says, well, bonjour, Fanny. So that nobody could-- and they said, how do you know the baron? I says, I know him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was interesting to see. I wanted to see what the chateau was like. Because I had heard the story later-- he told it to us-- that Goering, when the Germans occupied France, that this place was going to be for Goering. And it was to be left totally intact. And that's how the chateau was left intact. But the Rothschild family did an awful lot for Jewish children in France. Supported homes, and they did very good things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at this chateau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe close to a year because I also went to school from there at that point. You know, my uncle insisted that I go back to school. So I finished my high school quick with-- the ORT sent me to school. I'm sure you heard of ORT organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-R-T. And through them, I was able to finish high school. And then I started classes at the Sorbonne. And having to know-- in order to get in, I had to know another language. So I didn't tell-- so I took German. I figured out, well, I speak German. They don't have to know that I speak it. So that's why it was easier for me to take more classes. And I started to take classes then already in political science, because I always loved politics. And I think being around my uncle, especially my Uncle [INAUDIBLE], he was always very politically oriented. And I studied the Karl Marx theory, and-- so that was very fascinating for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question. Why did you go to live in the chateau rather than with your uncles in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was better for me because right after the war, it was-- everything was rationing. My uncles hadn't settled down. And it was-- they felt that it was better for me to be [? away. ?] It was very close to Paris. I mean, I could go in with the metro. It was, like, 20 minutes from Paris, you know. I wasn't away from Paris. Let's put it that way. Then eventually, I went back to Paris, and I wanted to visit my old foster family, the family [INAUDIBLE] that I had left-- who had left me that time in Clermont-Ferrand. Now, they had not heard from me, I hadn't heard from them. So I told my uncle that I wanted to visit them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there, and they were very pleased to see me, that I survived, because they didn't know. And he said to me, you know, it's ironic that you arrived today because yesterday I received a letter from the Red Cross. Because all families that were fostering Jewish children-- the Red Cross had their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they asked me to give you a name if I knew what happened to you. Because parents were looking for their children who were sent to homes, to families. They didn't know what happened to their kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, tomorrow I'm going to send your name, that you're back with us in case your mother or father are looking for you. So the next day, I go to the post office, because in the little village, you pick up your mail. And I noticed a postcard. And I felt so bad, I don't know, that I lost that postcard. That was written in French, but with my mom's signature. I recognized my mother's signature. And I knew my mom didn't speak French because she'd lived in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wrote to the family asking where I was. That she's looking for me. So immediately I called Paris, my uncles, and told them that mother is [? there and ?] there. What happened was when she was liberated in Theresienstadt, they ask her-- and probably all the other the Dutch group-- where they wanted to go back to. They said they wanted to go back to Amsterdam. Because she told my father if we are ever separated, let's meet back in Amsterdam. So she went back to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the postcard was the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was the first I'd heard from my mother since 1943. I hadn't seen my mother since 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew nothing about what happened to her from Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. And at that point, I became suspicious because my father's name, signature, wasn't on there. But I was really excited about just hearing from my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my uncle, within a week or so, went to Amsterdam to visit my mother. And preparing to make a visa to bring her to Paris. So at that point, I went back to Paris and started my school up again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the day arrived that my mother was coming to Paris. And my uncle went-- you know, was in Amsterdam with her. And I was with my other uncle. We prepared the apartment and everything. I remember we bought beautiful flowers. And I didn't know what to expect-- how my mother would look, what she was like. I knew she was in camp. I almost felt like a little, old, broken-down lady. I don't know what to think. She arrived and she looked quite well-- very well put together. She wore a beautiful hat, I remember. She looked well. There was only one problem-- having lived in France for all these years, and I blocked out my German. I wanted no more-- I didn't want to remember German. I was French. I became so French that I couldn't talk to her. I couldn't talk to her for about two days. And I spoke in French, and my uncle translated it into German. And my mother could understand that because I explained what happened. She was fully aware of it, and she didn't mind. And then after a couple days, hearing this German around me, it all came back. And so from that point on, we talked and we talked, and we had a lot of talking, and a lot of things to remember. And I remember then right away we called Los Angeles. She talked to my sister who was here in Los Angeles with her foster parents, you know. And so it was a wonderful reunion. And I was very lucky, I felt, that I had at least my mother back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you hear of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my mother found out that that whole Dutch transport-- this is, by the way, an interesting story. When my parents were in Theresienstadt--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we need to really go back to when they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: To when they had arrived in Theresienstadt. You know, they lived in barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when they went to Amsterdam, they were taken from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Amsterdam they were sent to a camp that was called Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I don't know what part of Holland. It was away from Amsterdam. I don't know where. I've never been there. Anyway, they were there in the camp for quite some years, and then they were sent to Theresienstadt. And when they called my father to report for work, they gave him his striped uniform. And my mother decided that she wants to go with him. She says, I'm going and work with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was walking down with her to the camp commandant, or whoever, and a gentleman appeared, my mother says, out of nowhere. And he came to my father, and in German, he says, to him, [GERMAN], which means almost like, sir, gentlemen, where are you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, well, my wife wants go with me on this work patrol. And he turned to him and says, please don't take her. And my father walked mother back to the barracks. And mother told me that story. She never saw that man before. He never saw him after. God only knows who it was. I don't know. But in essence, he saved my mother's life [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because my father didn't know he was going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: He thought he was going on a work patrol.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they told him. And my mother told me then that that whole Dutch transport was-- they put them in the gas chambers right away. They never came out. They killed them immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she remember when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, think that was October '44. And it was shortly before the war was going to be over in Europe. So it was ironic. And I survived all these years. And then he, you know, died in Auschwitz so late into the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother wrote to the Red Cross. And for some reason, the Polish-- even was written from Poland that this and this transport, number whatever-- they had the number-- was never heard from again, these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So naturally, we surmise. And I lost all the rest of my family-- my aunts, my uncles. They were all sent to Poland-- back to Poland from Germany. And none of them came back. So, to me, it was always like somebody had to come back from the family to perpetuate the family. And maybe that's one of the reasons-- because I was always very close to my father-- that I never gave up my maiden name, because I'd be the last one. So I never gave that up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and that's the way, you know, I feel. So I really feel I was a very lucky person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you reunited with your mother. And she survived in Theresienstadt? Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt is in Czechoslovakia, not too far from Prague. And it was a camp. My mother told me stories that they had to work in a factory, and the factory was called the [? Glimmer. ?] What they did-- they did raw material for the Germans, for airplanes. They worked on plastics. It was horrible, she told me. That they had to get up early in the morning, at 4 o'clock in the morning, and they walk a few miles to the factory. And that's why I'm so amazed how so many of [? you ?] people survived the camp. I mean, I was younger. I went underground. I bypassed it, but yet I witnessed it. I used to see train loads go by with people. And it's nothing I could say. I couldn't reach out to them. I would see them going by in France, and it's nothing I could do, because I felt I have to save myself. And the Jewish religion teaches that to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's interesting-- after the war, I was asked a question about my religion. They say, well, you still believe in God after all what is happening? Because I know of people that, shortly after the war, they had children. They would not circumcise their children. They didn't want to have-- they were afraid that maybe another Hitler would come or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I never felt that way. As a matter of fact, it made me a stronger religious person. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It never took my religion away from me. Because I was asked that by my rabbi here, and by other people. I says, uh-uh. I really became almost a stronger Jew because of what I witnessed and what I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- let's go back for a moment to when you were reunited with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I stayed in Paris with her-- I stayed about, oh, about a couple more years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in-- I went to school and I lived in a home near Paris. I went to school. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: With your mother? Did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother lived with my uncles, but I would come in. Sometimes I would stay over at night. And then we started to proceed with papers. My cousin in New York tried to arrange affidavit for me. And in 19-- she got the papers for me in 1947. And the end of '47-- that's when I came to the States, the late '47, '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came on the boat. Yes, I came alone. It was interesting--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in the student boat, and the boat [LAUGHS] was called the Marine Tiger. It took, from Le Havre to New York, 11 days. And there were people there that I knew-- girls that I knew from-- I even met two of them that I knew from Cologne. And then there was a group of Quakers, and it was mostly a student boat. We just had a wonderful time on that ship. We had our own newspaper.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only problem I had-- it was an American ship. I couldn't speak any English. And the steward, one of the stewards-- a very big, black man. And he noticed that I kept ordering roast beef every day. And he said to me, don't you want something else? So he finally decided he was going to take over and bring me different food because I couldn't understand the menu. But it was-- like I say, it took 10 days-- 11 days to arrive in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we arrived in New York-- I want to go back for a little while to Paris. The first American tourists I met in Paris-- I was not too impressed. The soldiers that I had met were really wonderful. I mean, they were great. I liked the American soldiers. When I met the first American tourist, it was always like, it wouldn't be for us. We helped liberate you. And I almost kind of resented that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't feel so great about coming to the States. I knew I had to come, but, I mean, I wanted to stay. You want to stop here? Let me just ask you, where did you first come when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where in New York?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my aunt, I remember, on West 91st Street near Riverside Drive there. It was-- then it was a nice area then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with my aunt. And it was also a funny thing. She had some friends over for coffee, and somebody asked me, tell me, is it really true what happened in Europe?
